title: Array Stability Validator with Incorrect Shift

statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, determine if it has at least one subarray of a specified size where all elements are in non-decreasing order. A subarray is considered stable if all elements are in non-decreasing order and has the same offset from the start of the array.
statement.inputFormat: First line contains an integer, the size of the subarray to be checked. Second line contains the array of integers.
statement.outputFormat: True if there exists a stable subarray with the specified size, False otherwise.
statement.notes: Time and space complexity must be within O(n) complexity.

constraints.description: Size of the subarray to be checked

examples.explanation: Example 1 subarray [2,3,4] is a stable subarray with size 3.
There does not exist any stable subarray with size 4.

hints.text: Think about two pointers approach to solve the problem.
To check the stability of subarray, you need to compare adjacent elements.

editorial.title: Editorial - Stability Validator
editorial.intuition: To determine the stability of a subarray, we can use a sliding window approach to slide through the subarray and compare adjacent elements.
editorial.bruteForce: We can simply use nested loops to check all possible subarrays.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a two pointers approach to find all subarrays of the specified size.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can maintain a counter to track the number of elements that are smaller than the current element. If the counter is equal to 0, we have found a stable subarray.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The two pointers approach will ensure that we visit every possible subarray exactly once. The counter will be 0 when we enter a stable subarray.
commonMistakes: Not correctly using the two pointers approach

functionSignature.python: def isStableSubarray(self, size: int, nums: list[int]) -> bool:

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def isStableSubarray(self, size, nums):
        if size == 1: return True
        count = 0
        for i in range(size-1, len(nums)):
            if nums[i] >= nums[i-1]: count = 0
            else: count += 1
            if count > 0: return False
        return True

# test cases
nums = [4, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 3, 1, 9, 8]
size = 3
print(Solution().isStableSubarray(size, nums))
